prompt_md: What does `= 0` at the end of a virtual function declaration mean?

options.a: The function returns zero by default when not overridden.
options.b: The function is pure virtual: the class is abstract and derived classes must implement it.
options.c: The function is deleted and cannot be called.
options.d: The function is assigned a default implementation of returning zero.

explanation_md: `= 0` marks a pure virtual function. It means the base class declares the contract but provides no implementation. Any class containing such a function becomes abstract and cannot be instantiated directly. Derived classes must override it to become concrete.
`speak` takes a `const Animal&`, so virtual dispatch applies. `c.sound()` returns `"meow"` and `d.sound()` returns `"woof"`. Each is printed on its own line. There is no trailing blank line.

prompt_md: Which of the following is an interface class in C++?

options.a: A class that has at least one virtual function.
options.b: A class with data members and pure virtual functions.
options.c: A class consisting entirely of pure virtual functions and a virtual destructor, with no data members.
options.d: A class whose constructor is private so external code cannot create it directly.

explanation_md: An interface class contains only pure virtual functions (plus a virtual destructor) and no data members or regular member functions. It describes capabilities without carrying state. Options (a) and (b) describe abstract classes with possible extras; option (d) describes a different pattern entirely.

`p` is a `Base*` pointing at a `Child` object. Because `greet` is virtual, dispatch goes to `Child::greet`, which prints `hello`. The newline is included but trailing whitespace is stripped by the checker.

prompt_md: A class `Derived` inherits from abstract base `Base` but does not override `Base`'s pure virtual function `compute()`. What happens when you write `Derived d;`?

options.a: It compiles fine; `compute()` just returns zero.
options.b: It compiles fine; calling `d.compute()` causes a runtime error.
options.c: Compile error: `Derived` is still abstract because `compute()` is not overridden.
options.d: Linker error: the vtable for `Derived` is missing an entry.

explanation_md: If a derived class fails to override every pure virtual function it inherits, it remains abstract. Attempting to instantiate it is a compile error, not a linker or runtime error. The error message will name `compute` as the unimplemented function.
